questionBank.question: If x=t^2 and y=t^3, then dy/dx=
questionBank.notesSnippet: For parametric curves, divide derivatives with respect to t.

questionBank.question: For parametric x(t),y(t), d^2y/dx^2 equals:
questionBank.explanation: Second derivative in parametric form divides by dx/dt again.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Apply chain rule with respect to x via t.

questionBank.question: Area enclosed by r = f(θ) from θ = a to θ = b is:
questionBank.explanation: Polar area = (1/2) int_a^b r^2 dθ; do not omit the 1/2 or the square on r.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Use theta bounds and square r.

questionBank.question: If x = r cos θ and y = r sin θ, then dy/dx =
questionBank.explanation: Differentiate x and y with respect to theta then divide.
questionBank.notesSnippet: Polar slope parallels parametric slope idea.

questionBank.question: If r = 2 on 0 ≤ θ ≤ π, area is:
questionBank.notesSnippet: Constant radius still uses polar area formula.

frqBank.title: Unit 9 FRQ - Parametric and Polar
frqBank.context: A curve is given parametrically by x=t^2+1, y=t^3-3t.

parts.label: (a)
parts.question: Find dy/dx at t=2.
parts.scoring: 1 pt derivatives, 1 pt substitution, 1 pt ratio.

parts.label: (b)
parts.question: Find equation of tangent line at t=2.
parts.scoring: 1 pt point, 1 pt slope, 1 pt equation.

9.1.title: 9.1 Parametric Basics
9.1.content:
FUN-8 · BC Parametric

Parametric curve

A curve is given by x = x(t), y = y(t). The parameter t might be time; the path can loop or reverse — t is not the x-coordinate.

dy/dx = (dy/dt) / (dx/dt)   provided dx/dt ≠ 0

Tangent lines & horizontal/vertical

Point: (x(t₀), y(t₀)). Slope = dy/dx above. Horizontal tangent when dy/dt = 0 and dx/dt ≠ 0; vertical when dx/dt = 0 and dy/dt ≠ 0.

Arc length (BC): ∫ √((dx/dt)² + (dy/dt)²) dt  ·  Speed = √((dx/dt)² + (dy/dt)²)
AP tip: Simplify dy/dx before evaluating at t₀; watch for where dx/dt = 0.

9.2.title: 9.2 Higher Parametric Derivatives
9.2.content:
FUN-8 · Parametric concavity

Second derivative d²y/dx²

First compute dy/dx as a function of t. Then

d²y/dx² = (d/dt)(dy/dx) ÷ (dx/dt)

Do not “square” the first derivative. This is a quotient of derivatives with respect to t.

Interpretation

Sign of d²y/dx² tells concavity of the y-vs-x graph along the path (how the curve bends in the plane).

AP tip: Differentiate (dy/dx) with respect to t using quotient/product rules as needed, then divide by dx/dt.

9.3.title: 9.3–9.4 Polar Area and Slope
9.3.content:
FUN-8 · BC Polar

Polar ↔ rectangular

x = r cos θ,   y = r sin θ   with r = f(θ)

Differentiate x and y with respect to θ using product rule; then dy/dx = (dy/dθ)/(dx/dθ).

Area bounded by one polar graph

Area = ½ ∫αβ r² dθ

Use when the region is swept by a single r = f(θ) between rays θ = α and θ = β. For loops and petals, choose α, β so you trace the boundary once.

Strategy

Sketch r vs θ or the polar curve; identify symmetry to reduce the integral. Watch for negative r (reflection through origin).

AP tip: Double-check θ-bounds against the graph — especially roses, limaçons, and spirals.
